item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I presume that that's the favored genre of the old woman who it was born from.

item.title: Re: My thoughts
item.content:encoded: You don't have to listen to me… I'm the author, so I'm dead.

item.title: My thoughts
item.content:encoded:
I know the author said what the secret backstory is and why this skip does what it does.
But… I got the impression of a kicked out fictional character. When talking about worlds in books, I got the idea of a character loosing his world once it was retold or rebooted. Imagine like a 60s era Batman no longer being in comics simply because HIS home is no longer there, but a gritty rebooted version with its OWN gritty batman. So now he blocks others from "reading", hopping his original home can be made real or "cannon" again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why do they read it murder stories? No vote until that is explained.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I wanted to upvote just for the mind-blowing implication that it's already eaten a bunch of words permanently and the entire English language is missing chunks we can't comprehend.

But I kept expecting an explanation as to why they read shit to it and… nothing came. It should really be a safe class since it doesn't even need to be fed or cleaned. You could just bind it in chains and stick it in a fuckin' crate really. Neutral.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … I don't actually think you can. That text isn't dialogue so it has to abide by the scientific standards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because

I can

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why does the interview use yards instead of meters?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is that guy in the picture wearing my a fedora? Guys I think he's wearing a fedora.
Reading through this discussion, I do have one concern. What I got from the article was not at all what the author intended. The idea that he was supposed to be a part of that woman's conciosness was not remotely clear to me when reading the article. But what I did understand correctly was a pretty good read, so take your upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also do not understand the reasoning behind reading murder stories to it, or any stories at all, or why it is in the dark and blindfolded. What happens if you just leave it sitting in a locked room? If the answer is "it's area of effect increases if you don't do X to keep it happy," I'll be heavily leaning toward downvoting because that equine corpse doesn't need another flogging.

Additionally, why would the old woman's family take away her books, other than to be jerks? Does the (blurred) sight of books that she now can't read drive her to murderous rage? Are they neglectful and/or illiterate enough that they can't be bothered to visit and read to her once in a while? Are they so destitute that they can't even buy a few thrift-store audio books to toss her way to keep her happy and out of their hair?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm. Alright. Was probably just the computer I was using.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Is the rating module on this fucked up for anyone else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Downvoted for this reason. Sooner or later the Foundation will run out of texts, or he'll get lucky and memorize a passage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh. I didn't find the concept very interesting. Also, why is the Foundation reading books to it? Wouldn't it be better to just not let it comprehend any form of language?

-1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Once per day, a research staff member should read SCP-2969 a literary passage detailing a murder, via the radio.

Why murder? I mean, he looks like a classical noir detective, so it fits, but nothing in the description gives any indication of why it wants murder stories.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to like this… but… I just don't. I find the effect is very confusing, and I don't see why the motivation given leads to this behaviour. I found the log to be a classic exercise in 'Interviewer asks direct questions, interviewee provides tangential, confusing, roundabout answers' which I found just irritating and I couldn't find the motivation to read it all. I don't understand why it's a man from a hardboiled black-and-white film, and I don't understand why we read it murder stories - in fact, I don't understand why we read to it at all, and why those things we've read to it then need to be stored somewhere it can't get to them. I also don't understand why we keep it blindfolded and in the dark. Altogether the containment procedures seem bizarre and arbitrary and lack information on potentially useful information such as feeding.

Sorry, roget :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You'd need a top-tier argument to convince him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"In Your Own Words," if you're still looking for title suggestions.

Is the language theft a conscious choice? Could he be persuaded to refrain from doing it to somebody if he were convinced that they respected stories as much as him?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's like a piece of her that broke off, he can be independent but he will never be whole.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this thing. I love what he does and how he does it and why he does it. I love it.

That being said, I'm interested, I think, in what if any connection he still has to the old woman from whence he came. If he is a part of her psyche that grew wings and took off, is he still an independent entity, or does he die if she dies?

Not that those questions need answering in the article, but it is something worth considering.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks for clearing that up. Heartily up voted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed.

Yes. It's taking the language away from them.

It likes them, makes it easier to contain if we do something it likes.

spoilers I guess
phhohphph

The old lady got her books taken away by her family, and they stuck her in a trailer home. She was so frustrated, her mind split and a manifestation of that despair, 2969, popped out. He was all "hey people don't deserve to keep language if they're going to be abusing it" and now confiscates language where he can. he's got a low range initially cos being born form an old woman's head doesn't imbue a lot of power.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor nitpick: It said the interview was conducted from 500 meters away, but the description states that the AoE is 650-750 meters. Might wanna fix that.
I'm not sure I entirely understood this skip. From what I can tell, if it reads something, then people within the AoE cannot use any words it read from the book, nor can they read the book itself. Is that correct?
I also don't understand why we read murder stories to it.
The skip seems interesting, though, and if I am interpreting the whole blind nurse thing correctly, that adds to its intrigue. Would you mind explaining it just to clear it up for me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about… Literacy Defender?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does this apply to braille as well?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted - reminds me of some of the older entries, which I like. As for a name, I'd suggest something along the lines of "Thief of Words", but that might sound a bit cliché.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm happy with everything about this except the name, so if anyone has any names better than "Language Hoarder", leave 'em here please.

e: Also credit to Faminepulse for making the image for me. He is cool.
